prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. My boss wrote this for the company newsletter and asked for my thoughts. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Feedback: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

- **Strong narrative arc** — You move smoothly from setup through experience to reflection without unnecessary tangents. The pacing is just right for ~500 words.
- **Specific, concrete details** — "Pick and roll," the scrimmage moment, the kid telling you to stay out of the way — these details make the story land. They're not generic.
- **Genuine voice** — This feels honest and unguarded. You don't pretend the failure didn't hurt or try to spin it as secretly great. That makes the eventual lesson more credible.
- **Layered learning** — You don't just conclude "failure teaches you things." You identify *specific* lessons (casual vs. competitive play, failure as redirection, the "you don't know what you don't know" insight). The last one especially is sophisticated.
- **The debate pivot** — This is a nice touch because it shows failure actually *led somewhere better*, which is more interesting than just "I learned resilience."

## Room to Improve

- **Show more than tell** — "I was really upset for a while" is tell. What did that look like? Did you avoid the gym? Replay the scrimmage in your head? A concrete detail or two would hit harder.
- **The Marcus thread** — You mention him twice but don't fully develop how that stung. One more sentence exploring that comparison could deepen the emotional impact.
- **Sensory details** — The experience is mostly dialogue and action. What did the gym smell like? What did nerves feel like in your chest during the scrimmage? Small touches would make it more vivid.
- **Watch the casualness** — Phrases like "Obviously" and "So yeah" work for the conversational tone, but depending on the context, slightly tighter language might feel more polished.

## Bottom Line

This is a well-executed personal narrative with a genuine voice and real insight. It does exactly what the assignment asks. The main lift would be adding more sensory/emotional texture rather than changing structure or substance.

The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

## What Works Really Well

**Genuine narrative arc** — This has a clear, satisfying shape: overconfidence → reality check → redirection → reflection. It actually *goes somewhere*.

**Specific, concrete details** — "Pick and roll," the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team while you didn't, the pivot to debate. These ground the story and make it vivid.

**Authentic voice** — This reads like a real person writing, not someone trying to sound like an essay. That conversational tone makes it engaging.

**Real learning, not surface-level** — The lessons aren't generic. You identify something actually true: casual play ≠ competitive skill, and failure can redirect you toward a better fit. The insight about "you don't know what you don't know until it becomes obvious" is genuinely thoughtful.

**Clean writing** — Grammar is solid, sentences have good variety, and it moves smoothly.

## What Could Improve

**The reflection section is slightly repetitive** — You circle around the "don't know what you don't know" idea in the last three paragraphs. Tighten this; you've made the point.

**The debate pivot needs more development** — This is actually the most interesting part, but it's rushed. How did you land on debate specifically? Was there a moment that sparked it, or did you just try it? Even 2-3 more sentences would strengthen the narrative.

**More sensory detail in key moments** — The scrimmage scene is your most dramatic moment. What did it *feel* like being told to stay out of the way? More embodied detail would make it hit harder.

## Bottom Line

This is solid, honest work that fully answers the assignment. It's engaging and shows genuine self-reflection. With a bit of tightening in the reflection section and development of the debate pivot, this would be even stronger.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works

**Strong narrative structure** – The essay has excellent pacing, moving smoothly from overconfidence → reality check → humiliation → disappointment → resolution. You feel the arc.

**Specific, concrete details** – "Pick and roll," the scrimmage where someone tells him to stay out of the way, Marcus making the team – these make it vivid and credible rather than generic.

**Authentic voice** – This reads like a real person reflecting, not someone performing. The conversational tone ("so yeah, I failed") keeps it engaging.

**Genuine multiple lessons** – Goes beyond just "I learned I wasn't good enough" to include insights about casual vs. competitive play, self-awareness, and how failure can redirect you toward better fits.

**Solid length** – Hits the ~500-word target.

## What Could Be Stronger

**The debate team pivot is underdeveloped** – This is actually the most interesting part of the story (the "something else that was better"), but it gets only a paragraph. How did you decide on debate? What was that first practice like? This deserves more space.

**Telling instead of showing** – Phrases like "I was really upset for a while" miss an opportunity to *show* that disappointment through a scene or image. The best moments are already shown (the scrimmage), so lean into that more.

**The ending gets slightly generic** – "Failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" is true but a bit polished/clichéd for an otherwise genuine essay. The "you don't know what you don't know" observation is stronger—lean on that instead.

## Suggestion
Trim something from the basketball section and expand the debate pivot. That's where the real payoff is.
